writing_task: I need to email a key customer — Priya at Northfield Logistics — letting her know that a feature she requested six months ago is finally shipping next week. She'll need to coordinate with her IT team to enable it, so there's a clear action item on her end.

drafts.rubric:
Subject: Route Optimization feature shipping next week — action needed from your IT team

Priya,

Six months is a long time to wait on something you built a workflow around. The Route Optimization feature ships on Tuesday, and before you can use it, your IT team will need to enable it on your end — that's a real coordination step, not just a toggle.

Here's the sequence: feature goes live Tuesday the 14th. Your IT team needs to enable it in your admin settings before your users will see it. If they hit anything unexpected, reach out to me directly and I'll loop in our implementation team same day.

— [Your name]
drafts.generic:
Subject: Your Requested Feature Is Shipping Next Week

Hi Priya,

Great news — the feature you requested six months ago is finally ready and will be rolling out next week. We're excited to get this into your hands after the wait.

There is one action item on your end before you can take full advantage of it: your IT team will need to enable the feature within your account settings. I'd recommend looping them in now so everything is ready to go on launch day.

Please don't hesitate to reach out if you have questions. Looking forward to hearing your feedback once it's live.

Best,
drafts.preference:
Subject: Your Requested Feature Is Shipping Next Week

Hi Priya,

Quick update — the feature you requested six months ago is finally ready and shipping next week. We know you've been waiting on this one, so we wanted to make sure you heard it from us first.

There's one thing you'll need to do on your end: your IT team will need to enable the feature once it's live. It won't activate automatically, so looping them in before next week will help you hit the ground running.

Can you confirm you've connected with your IT team by Friday? Happy to answer any questions in the meantime.

Thanks,
[Your name]

user_reason: A hits the right structure — acknowledges the wait, delivers the news, explains why the IT coordination matters from Priya's perspective (hitting the ground running), and lands on a concrete deadline with a clear owner. The CTA is clean and forward-looking. C has good energy and specificity, and I like that it names the feature and treats the coordination step seriously, but the closing is too prescriptive about org structure (admin settings, implementation team) and it skips the emotional acknowledgment before diving into logistics — it feels abrupt. B is the weakest: 'Great news' and 'We're excited' center us, not Priya, the action item is vague, and 'Please don't hesitate to reach out' followed by 'Looking forward to hearing your feedback' is exactly the trailing sentiment that kills momentum after a CTA.

criteria_analysis.name: Reader-Centered Justification
criteria_analysis.reasoning: Draft C opens by acknowledging Priya's reality — she's been waiting six months and built a workflow around this — before delivering the action item, which is exactly the cost-before-rationale ordering this user values. Drafts A and B mention the wait but treat it as a throwaway detail rather than centering the reader's experience, and neither names the specific burden the coordination step imposes.

criteria_analysis.name: Purposeful Openings
criteria_analysis.reasoning: Draft C's opening names the situation immediately with reader-centered framing and doesn't echo the subject line — it adds context the subject line doesn't carry. Draft A opens with 'Quick update' filler and then essentially restates the subject line. Draft B leads with 'Great news' sentiment padding and similarly echoes the subject line content.

criteria_analysis.name: Operational Closings
criteria_analysis.reasoning: Draft C closes with a concrete sequence (Tuesday the 14th, IT enables in admin settings, escalation path directly to the sender with same-day implementation team loop-in) — no trailing filler. Draft A closes on an action (confirm by Friday) but then adds 'Happy to answer any questions' filler. Draft B dissolves entirely into sentiment ('Looking forward to hearing your feedback'), which is exactly the pattern this user rejects.

criteria_analysis.name: Direct, Human Tone
criteria_analysis.reasoning: Draft C reads like a person talking — 'that's a real coordination step, not just a toggle' is crisp, specific, and conversational. Draft A is mostly clean but leans on stock phrasing ('hit the ground running,' 'Happy to answer any questions'). Draft B uses multiple boilerplate phrases ('Please don't hesitate to reach out,' 'Looking forward to hearing your feedback,' 'We're excited') that this user's rubric specifically flags.

criteria_analysis.name: Lean Information Density
criteria_analysis.reasoning: Draft C is the leanest — every sentence either delivers a milestone, an action item, or an escalation path. It names a specific date (Tuesday the 14th), a specific location (admin settings), and a specific escalation channel. Draft A is reasonably lean but the 'won't activate automatically' sentence explains process the reader doesn't need. Draft B adds unnecessary explanation ('within your account settings,' 'take full advantage of it') and pads with sentiment that doesn't earn its place.

per_criterion.reasoning: Draft C opens by acknowledging Priya's reality — she's been waiting six months and built a workflow around this — before delivering the action item, which is exactly the cost-before-rationale ordering this user values. Drafts A and B mention the wait but treat it as a throwaway detail rather than centering the reader's experience, and neither names the specific burden the coordination step imposes.

rubric_judge_result.overall_reasoning: Draft C aligns with this user's demonstrated preferences across every criterion: it centers the reader's reality before the ask, opens without filler or subject-line echo, closes on a concrete escalation path with no trailing sentiment, reads like a person talking, and carries zero wasted detail. Draft A is functional but generic; Draft B accumulates the exact anti-patterns (sentiment padding, stock phrases, soft closings) this user consistently eliminates.

per_criterion.reasoning: Draft A provides a specific date (Tuesday the 14th), spells out the exact sequence of steps, and offers a concrete escalation path. Draft B is clear but vaguer — 'next week' and 'account settings' are less precise.
Draft A flows in a tight logical sequence: context → timeline → action → fallback. Draft B follows a reasonable flow but spends more time on pleasantries that slightly dilute the logical progression.
Both drafts are free of grammatical errors and typos, with proper punctuation and sentence construction throughout.

Both are well-organized short emails. Draft A uses a clear paragraph-per-purpose structure; Draft B similarly separates news, action item, and closing, though its structure is slightly more conventional.

Draft A's opening line ('Six months is a long time to wait') is direct and acknowledges the reader's experience in a way that grabs attention. Draft B relies on stock phrases like 'Great news' and 'We're excited,' which feel generic and less engaging.

Draft A strikes a confident, competent, peer-to-peer professional tone that respects the reader's time. Draft B leans on corporate pleasantries ('don't hesitate to reach out,' 'looking forward to hearing your feedback') that feel formulaic and slightly impersonal.

generic_judge_result.overall_reasoning: Draft A is more specific, more direct, and more engaging while maintaining full professionalism. It gives the reader exactly what they need (date, steps, escalation path) without filler, and its tone feels authentic rather than templated.

suggestion_text:
- **Reader-Centered Justification**: The original preference draft outscored the rubric-guided draft here because it named the specific burden (six months of waiting, a workflow built around the feature) before asking for anything — the rubric's description covers the ordering principle but doesn't push hard enough on naming the *specific* reader context (history, dependencies, what's already in motion). Tightened the description and added a dimension to surface pre-existing commitments or dependencies the reader has already built around the situation.
- **Purposeful Openings**: The original preference draft outscored the rubric-guided draft because its opening added context the subject line didn't carry, while the rubric-guided draft restated the subject line with filler. The existing dimensions are correct but the description undersells the positive standard — added language clarifying that the opening should *add* framing the subject line doesn't already contain, not just avoid repeating it.
- **Operational Closings**: The original preference draft outscored the rubric-guided draft significantly here — the rubric-guided draft softened its CTA with trailing filler after a clean deadline ask, which the user explicitly called out. The criterion is directionally right but needs a dimension that flags the pattern of appending open-ended offers ('happy to answer questions') after a concrete CTA, since that's the specific failure mode the rubric-guided draft exhibited.
- **Direct, Human Tone**: The original preference draft outscored the rubric-guided draft because it used a specific, unexpected phrase ('that's a real coordination step, not just a toggle') that signals someone actually thinking about the reader's situation rather than filling space. The existing criterion covers what to avoid but undersells the positive standard — added a dimension encouraging specific, situation-anchored language over generic reassurance.
- **Lean Information Density**: The original preference draft outscored the rubric-guided draft because every sentence carried a milestone, an action, or an escalation path — nothing explained process the reader already understood. The criterion is correct and its dimensions are sound; kept unchanged since the gap here is captured better by strengthening Reader-Centered Justification and Operational Closings, not by modifying this criterion.

suggested_rubric.name: Reader-Centered Justification
suggested_rubric.description: When explaining a decision or making an ask, frame it from where the audience is sitting — including what they've already built, committed to, or been waiting on. Name the specific costs or dependencies they carry before making the case. The 'why' should land from their perspective, not from the sender's. Ordering matters: impact first, rationale second.

dimensions.label: Names specific disruptions or burdens the audience will face as a result of the decision
Acknowledgment of impact comes before the justification, not after
Reasoning is framed in terms of consequences for the reader, not the sender's standards or values
Tone reads as candid acknowledgment rather than minimizing the burden or asking the reader to feel good about it
Where relevant, names pre-existing commitments, workflows, or context the reader has already built around the situation

suggested_rubric.name: Purposeful Openings
suggested_rubric.description: Openings should name the situation immediately without filler — no 'announcing that an announcement is coming.' The opening should add framing the subject line doesn't already carry, not restate it. Before delivering hard news, one orienting sentence gives the reader a beat of footing so the message feels like a person talking, not a subject line repeated.

dimensions.label: Opening does not waste a sentence announcing that information is about to follow
The core situation or context is named within the first two sentences
Before hard news, a brief framing sentence eases the reader in without feeling like a preamble
Opening doesn't read as a restatement of the subject line
Opening adds framing or context the subject line doesn't already contain — it advances the reader's understanding rather than restating what they already know

suggested_rubric.name: Operational Closings
suggested_rubric.description: The email should land on concrete next steps, deadlines, and accountability — not dissolve into gratitude or motivation. The reader should walk away knowing exactly what happens next. Once a concrete CTA is stated, the message ends — open-ended offers or soft follow-up invitations after the CTA dilute it.

dimensions.label: Final section ends on specific actions, dates, or owners — not sentiment or thanks
Escalation path or point of contact is stated explicitly, not left implied
No motivational filler or gratitude padding appears after the operational close
Key milestones or deadlines are compressed into a tight closing sequence the reader retains
Does not append open-ended offers ('happy to answer questions,' 'feel free to reach out') after a concrete CTA — the action item is the last thing the reader sees

suggested_rubric.name: Direct, Human Tone
suggested_rubric.description: The message should read like a person talking, not a corporate memo. Avoid stock phrases and sentiment padding. Use plain, specific language and crisp comparative framing over hedged or abstract corporate vocabulary. The strongest signals of human tone are situation-specific word choices — language that could only have been written about this reader's situation, not recycled from any other email.

dimensions.label: Avoids boilerplate phrases like 'I wanted to reach out,' 'thank you for your flexibility,' or 'please don't hesitate'
Uses concrete, plain language rather than abstract or padded corporate vocabulary
Key trade-offs or decisions are framed in clean, comparative terms rather than hedged explanations
Reads as someone actually talking to colleagues, not issuing a formal notice
At least one specific, situation-anchored phrase signals that the writer is thinking about this reader's context — not filling space with generic reassurance

suggested_rubric.name: Lean Information Density
suggested_rubric.description: Timelines and action items should read as a sequence of checkpoints — dates and milestones only. Strip role descriptions, process explanations, and details the audience already knows. Anything that doesn't sharpen clarity is noise that dilutes the signal.

dimensions.label: Action items list dates and outcomes, not descriptions of how the work gets done
Does not explain team roles or responsibilities the audience already understands
Ownership is consolidated to a single person or channel rather than scattered across roles
Every piece of supporting detail either clarifies the next step or is cut

writing_task: Help me write an email to our head of sales letting him know we're killing a feature that his team has been demoing to prospects — we discovered it creates a security liability and can't ship it. He needs to update his team's pitch decks before the conference next Thursday.

drafts.rubric:
Subject: Feature removal before Thursday's conference

I know your team has been actively demoing this in prospect conversations, so I want to give you as much runway as possible.

We're pulling [feature name] from the product — it won't ship. Security review flagged it as a liability we can't work around, and continuing to demo it would mean walking prospects into a commitment we can't honor.

That puts your team in a rough spot, especially with the conference next Thursday and decks already in circulation.

Please have your team's pitch decks updated to remove the feature before Thursday morning.
drafts.generic:
Subject: Urgent: Feature Removal Before Conference – Action Required

Hi [Name],

I need to flag an urgent issue. We've discovered that the [feature name] your team has been demoing to prospects creates a significant security liability, so we've made the decision not to ship it. I know this is difficult news given your pipeline activity.

To protect us in front of prospects, your team's pitch decks need to be updated before the conference next Thursday. Please remove any references to this feature from all sales materials as soon as possible.

I'm happy to jump on a call today to discuss talking points. Let me know what works.

[Your Name]
drafts.preference:
Subject: Action Required: Remove [Feature Name] from Sales Decks Before Thursday

Hey [Name],

We've had to pull [feature name] from the roadmap — our security team flagged it as a liability we can't ship around.

I know your team has been demoing it to prospects, so I wanted to get ahead of this before the conference. The last thing either of us wants is someone promising something we can't deliver.

Can you make sure the team updates their pitch decks by end of day Wednesday? Happy to jump on a quick call if you need help with messaging around the change.

Thanks,
[Your name]

user_reason: A nails the arc — acknowledges the situation briefly, delivers the news, frames the stakes from his perspective ('promising something we can't deliver'), and ends with a concrete owner/deadline plus an offer to help. It's tight without feeling cold. C has good bones and the stakes framing is solid, but the closing line is just a deadline with no forward motion — it trails off instead of closing cleanly, and the opener is a bit too soft. B falls apart on tone and structure: 'I need to flag an urgent issue' centers me, not him; 'to protect us' is sender-perspective reasoning; 'please remove any references from all sales materials as soon as possible' is vague and slightly passive-aggressive in urgency without a real deadline anchor. Also 'I know this is difficult news' reads as filler sympathy.

criteria_analysis.name: Emotional Arc and Sequencing
criteria_analysis.reasoning: Draft C follows the exact prescribed sequence: acknowledges the reader's effort first ('I know your team has been actively demoing this'), then delivers the news ('We're pulling [feature name]'), then explains why it matters ('That puts your team in a rough spot, especially with the conference'), then closes with one concrete next step. Draft A leads with the news before the acknowledgment (acknowledgment comes second sentence), breaking the sequence. Draft B opens with 'I need to flag an urgent issue' which is sender-framed, not an acknowledgment of the reader's situation, and the sequence is further muddled by mixing news and rationale.

criteria_analysis.name: Reader-Centered Framing
criteria_analysis.reasoning: Draft C excels here — it names specific reader-side consequences ('walking prospects into a commitment we can't honor,' 'puts your team in a rough spot, especially with the conference next Thursday and decks already in circulation'). Draft A partially frames from the reader's perspective ('promising something we can't deliver') but the reasoning ('our security team flagged it') is sender-framed. Draft B uses 'To protect us' which is partially reader-framed but also centers the sender's decision-making ('we've made the decision'), and 'I know this is difficult news given your pipeline activity' is somewhat generic.

criteria_analysis.name: Clear, Single-Owner Action Items
criteria_analysis.reasoning: Draft C assigns ownership clearly ('your team's pitch decks') with a specific deadline ('before Thursday morning'). Draft A says 'by end of day Wednesday' which is a specific deadline and names 'the team' via the reader, scoring adequately but slightly less precisely. Draft B says 'as soon as possible' which is a vague timeframe rather than a specific deadline — a clear rubric violation — though 'before the conference next Thursday' appears earlier as context.

criteria_analysis.name: Lean Information Density
criteria_analysis.reasoning: Draft C is the leanest — every sentence earns its place, paragraphs are short, and there's no filler. Draft A is fairly tight but includes 'The last thing either of us wants is someone promising something we can't deliver' which is slightly padded. Draft B has redundancy ('I need to flag an urgent issue' plus the urgent subject line) and 'I know this is difficult news given your pipeline activity' adds a sentence that doesn't sharpen the action.

criteria_analysis.name: Operational Closings
criteria_analysis.reasoning: Draft C closes perfectly — the last line is the action with a deadline and nothing follows it. Draft A appends 'Happy to jump on a quick call if you need help with messaging' and then 'Thanks' after the CTA — both trailing filler and a soft offer, which this user's rubric explicitly flags as failures. Draft B similarly appends 'I'm happy to jump on a call today to discuss talking points. Let me know what works.' after the CTA, which is a soft open-ended offer post-action.

criteria_analysis.name: Direct, Human Tone
criteria_analysis.reasoning: Draft C reads like a person talking — 'walking prospects into a commitment we can't honor' and 'puts your team in a rough spot' are situation-anchored and human. Draft A is fairly conversational ('I wanted to get ahead of this before the conference') but slightly stock. Draft B reads more like a corporate notice — 'I need to flag an urgent issue,' 'we've made the decision,' 'To protect us in front of prospects' all feel formal and sender-centric rather than conversational.

rubric_judge_result.overall_reasoning: Draft C nails every criterion the user cares most about: perfect emotional sequencing (acknowledge → news → stakes → action), fully reader-centered framing, and a clean operational close with no trailing sentiment — exactly matching the user's demonstrated preferences from their conversation history. Draft A is decent but fails on the closing (soft offer + thanks after CTA) and slightly breaks the sequence. Draft B reads most corporate, uses vague deadlines, and closes on soft offers.

per_criterion.reasoning: Both drafts clearly communicate the core message: the feature is being pulled for security reasons, and decks must be updated before the conference. Neither leaves ambiguity about the action required.

Draft A flows in a tight logical sequence: context → decision + reason → impact acknowledgment → action. Draft B follows a similar arc but the phrase 'To protect us in front of prospects' introduces a slightly different framing mid-stream that feels less seamlessly connected.
Both drafts are free of grammatical errors, typos, and mechanical issues.

Draft A uses deliberate paragraph breaks to separate context, decision, empathy, and action into distinct beats, making it very scannable. Draft B is well-organized but slightly less crisp in its paragraph segmentation.

Draft A's opening line immediately hooks the reader by acknowledging their reality, and phrases like 'walking prospects into a commitment we can't honor' are vivid and compelling. Draft B is competent but more formulaic in its language.

Draft A strikes an impressive balance—direct and authoritative without being cold, empathetic without being apologetic. Draft B is appropriately professional but leans on conventional corporate email conventions ('flag an urgent issue,' 'happy to jump on a call') that make the voice less distinctive. The 'Urgent' and 'Action Required' subject line tags feel slightly heavy-handed.

generic_judge_result.overall_reasoning: Draft A is the stronger piece: it communicates the same information more concisely, with sharper prose, better structural rhythm, and a more confident yet empathetic tone. Draft B is perfectly competent but reads as a more conventional corporate email without the same level of craft.

suggestion_text:
- **Emotional Arc and Sequencing**: The original preference draft outscored the rubric-guided draft here, and the user's feedback confirms they valued the arc in the rubric-guided draft ('A nails the arc') even while ranking the original preference draft first overall — the gap is less about the sequence rule and more about how strictly the acknowledgment dimension is enforced. Tightened the acknowledgment dimension to make clear it must be reader-situation-oriented, not a sender-framed opener, since the rubric-guided draft opened with news rather than acknowledgment.
- **Reader-Centered Framing**: The original preference draft significantly outscored the rubric-guided draft, and the user's own commentary praised the original preference draft's stakes framing ('putting prospects into a commitment we can't honor') while calling out the generic draft's 'to protect us' as sender-perspective reasoning. Added a dimension explicitly prohibiting sender-organization reasoning (security team concerns, internal decisions) as the primary framing vehicle, since the rubric-guided draft defaulted to 'our security team flagged it' rather than centering the reader's exposure.
- **Clear, Single-Owner Action Items**: The original preference draft outscored the rubric-guided draft modestly, but the user praised the rubric-guided draft's deadline ('by end of day Wednesday') and didn't flag the action item as a problem — the gap here is small and within scoring noise. Kept unchanged.
- **Lean Information Density**: The original preference draft outscored the rubric-guided draft, but the user's feedback called the rubric-guided draft 'tight' and only flagged the closing as the structural problem — the density criterion itself is functioning correctly. Kept unchanged.
- **Operational Closings**: This is the sharpest inversion in the scores and directly contradicts the user's ranking: the rubric judge scored the rubric-guided draft a 1 here, yet the user ranked it second overall and praised it for ending with 'a concrete owner/deadline plus an offer to help.' The user's feedback reveals that a brief, functional offer to help (call to assist with messaging) is acceptable when it serves the action — the criterion is currently too absolute in prohibiting any post-CTA language. Updated the description and dimensions to distinguish between open-ended soft offers ('feel free to reach out') and brief, action-adjacent offers that reduce friction on the concrete ask, so rubric-guided drafts aren't penalized for the latter.
- **Direct, Human Tone**: The original preference draft outscored the rubric-guided draft, but the user praised the rubric-guided draft's tone ('tight without feeling cold') and didn't flag tone as a failure — the criterion is working adequately. Kept unchanged.

suggested_rubric.name: Emotional Arc and Sequencing
suggested_rubric.description: Information must follow a specific order: acknowledge the situation or effort first (one sentence max), then deliver the news, then explain why it matters, then one concrete next step. The opening acknowledgment must be oriented toward the reader's situation — not a sender-framed statement like 'I need to flag an issue.' Violating this sequence feels wrong even if all the pieces are present. This isn't just about completeness — it's about the reader feeling oriented before they're asked to act.

dimensions.label: Opens with a brief, reader-situation-oriented acknowledgment — one sentence, not a paragraph, and not a sender-framed opener like 'I need to flag an issue'
The actual news or decision is stated before the explanation — not buried after rationale
Stakes or consequences are explained before the call to action, not after
Closes with exactly one concrete next step — not a list of options or an open invitation
All four elements appear in order: acknowledge → news → why it matters → next step

suggested_rubric.name: Reader-Centered Framing
suggested_rubric.description: When explaining a decision, delivering news, or making an ask, frame the stakes entirely from the reader's perspective — what they've committed to, planned around, waited for, or will now have to absorb. The sender's reasoning, standards, or internal processes should not be the primary frame. Referencing internal causes (e.g. 'our security team flagged it') is acceptable as brief context, but the consequence framing must return immediately to the reader's exposure, not stay on the sender's side. If the email centers the sender's perspective over the reader's situation, it reads as tone-deaf regardless of intent.

dimensions.label: Names specific consequences, stakes, or disruptions the reader faces — what they'll need to do, absorb, or coordinate
Reasoning and stakes are framed in terms of impact on the reader's world, not the sender's standards, values, or internal decisions

Does not use sender-side reasoning (internal team decisions, organizational standards) as the primary framing vehicle — these may appear briefly as context but consequences must be named from the reader's side
Tone reads as honest about the reader's situation — not minimizing the impact, overselling the upside, or asking the reader to feel good about it
Where relevant, names pre-existing commitments, timelines, or context the reader has already built around the situation

suggested_rubric.name: Clear, Single-Owner Action Items
suggested_rubric.description: When action is needed, name one clear owner with a deadline. Don't over-specify to a named individual when the recipient is better positioned to determine internal ownership — 'your IT team' is appropriate when you don't know their org structure. Group asks like 'everyone please review' are non-actions that diffuse accountability. The ask should be clear without being presumptuous about how the recipient's team works.

dimensions.label: Action item names a clear functional owner — a specific person if known, a functional group (e.g. 'your IT team') if not — rather than a diffuse group ask
The action item includes a specific deadline, not a vague timeframe
Avoids 'everyone please...' or other fully diffuse asks where no one feels accountable — a named functional team is acceptable

Does not assume or name internal org structure details the sender has no basis to know — prefers functional group references over invented point-person language when the sender doesn't know the recipient's structure

suggested_rubric.name: Lean Information Density
suggested_rubric.description: Strip anything that doesn't sharpen clarity. Timelines list dates and outcomes, not process explanations. Don't re-explain things the audience already knows. Short paragraphs. Every sentence earns its place.

dimensions.label: Action items list dates and outcomes, not descriptions of how the work gets done
Does not explain team roles or responsibilities the audience already understands
Every piece of supporting detail either clarifies the next step or is cut
Paragraphs are short — no walls of text, no multi-sentence blocks that could be split or cut

suggested_rubric.name: Operational Closings
suggested_rubric.description: The email ends on a concrete next step with a deadline and a clear owner. Once the CTA is stated, the email is over — no trailing sentiment, no 'thanks for your patience,' no open-ended offers to answer questions. The last thing the reader sees should be the action, not a feeling. A brief, action-adjacent offer (e.g. 'happy to jump on a call to work through the messaging') is acceptable immediately before or alongside the CTA if it directly reduces friction on the ask — but open-ended soft offers appended after a complete CTA are not.

dimensions.label: Final section ends on specific actions, dates, or owners — not sentiment or thanks
No motivational filler or gratitude padding appears after the operational close

Does not append open-ended offers ('feel free to reach out,' 'let me know if you have questions') after a concrete CTA — a brief, action-adjacent offer that supports the ask is acceptable if it appears before or alongside the CTA, not after it
Closing line is action-oriented and forward-looking — not backward-looking gratitude or encouragement

suggested_rubric.name: Direct, Human Tone
suggested_rubric.description: The message should read like a person talking, not a corporate memo. Avoid stock phrases, jargon, and sentiment padding. Use plain, specific language. The strongest signal of human tone is language that could only have been written about this reader's situation — not recycled from any other email.

dimensions.label: Avoids boilerplate phrases like 'I wanted to reach out,' 'thank you for your flexibility,' or 'please don't hesitate'
Uses concrete, plain language rather than abstract or padded corporate vocabulary
Free of corporate jargon — no 'synergy,' 'leverage,' 'circle back,' 'alignment,' or similar filler
Reads as someone actually talking to colleagues or contacts, not issuing a formal notice
At least one specific, situation-anchored phrase signals that the writer is thinking about this reader's context — not filling space with generic reassurance

writing_task: I need to email our engineering and design leads about a scope change on the dashboard redesign — the CEO wants us to add real-time alerts, which means the timeline shifts and one of the teams needs to own the new component. Help me draft something that gets both leads aligned without micromanaging who does what.

drafts.rubric:
Subject: Dashboard redesign — scope change and timeline shift

You're both deep into the current dashboard plan, so I want to give you a clear picture of what's changing before it hits your schedules.

The CEO has added real-time alerts as a required feature. That moves the delivery date, and one of your teams will need to own the alerts component end-to-end.

This affects the work you've already sequenced — particularly anything that was scoped around the original feature set and timeline. I'm not prescribing which team takes it; you're better placed to make that call than I am.

Let's meet Thursday by noon to confirm the revised timeline and settle who owns alerts.
drafts.generic:
Subject: Dashboard Redesign — Scope Update and Timeline Adjustment

Hi both,

Quick heads-up on a scope change coming from the CEO. We're adding real-time alerts to the dashboard redesign, which affects our current timeline and introduces a new component that needs an owner.

I'd like us to connect briefly this week to figure out how this fits into each team's workload and agree on who takes the lead on the alerts piece. I trust you both to sort out the best division of responsibility — I just want to make sure we're aligned before moving forward.

Can you share your availability for a short sync?

Thanks
drafts.preference:
Subject: Dashboard Redesign — Scope Update + Timeline Shift

Hey both,

Quick heads-up: the CEO has asked us to add real-time alerts to the dashboard redesign. It's a meaningful addition, so the timeline needs to shift and one team needs to take ownership of building the new component.

I'd rather work this out together than hand down a decision, so I want to get you both on a call this week.

Before that, can you each take a quick look at your current capacity and come ready to talk through ownership and a revised timeline? Reply with your availability and we'll get it scheduled.

user_reason: A is the closest to what I'd actually send. It acknowledges the situation first, delivers the news, frames the impact from their perspective (affects work they've already sequenced), defers ownership to them without being wishy-washy, and closes with a concrete action — specific owner, specific deadline. That Thursday-by-noon line is exactly the kind of forward-looking CTA I want. C is decent but 'I'd rather work this out together than hand down a decision' centers my reasoning, not their situation, and 'Reply with your availability' is a weaker close than naming the meeting window. B is the weakest — 'Can you share your availability for a short sync?' is a non-action dressed up as one, 'Thanks' is trailing sentiment with no momentum, and 'I trust you both to sort out the best division of responsibility' reads a little passive, like I'm stepping back instead of driving alignment.

criteria_analysis.name: Emotional Arc and Sequencing
criteria_analysis.reasoning: Draft A opens with a reader-oriented acknowledgment ('You're both deep into the current dashboard plan'), delivers the news, explains why it matters (affects sequenced work), then closes with one next step with a deadline. Draft C follows a similar arc but the acknowledgment is weaker ('Quick heads-up' is slightly sender-framed). Draft B buries the stakes — it jumps from news to asking for a meeting without clearly explaining why it matters to the readers' existing work before the ask, and the acknowledgment is a generic 'Quick heads-up' rather than reader-situation-oriented.

criteria_analysis.name: Reader-Centered Framing
criteria_analysis.reasoning: Draft A explicitly names the reader's disruption — 'This affects the work you've already sequenced — particularly anything that was scoped around the original feature set and timeline' — anchoring stakes in their world. Draft C mentions 'meaningful addition' and timeline shift but doesn't name specific reader-side consequences like disrupted sequencing. Draft B frames almost everything from a process/sender perspective ('I'd like us to connect,' 'I just want to make sure we're aligned') and never names what the readers will have to absorb or re-plan.

criteria_analysis.name: Operational Closings
criteria_analysis.reasoning: Draft A closes cleanly on action with a deadline: 'Let's meet Thursday by noon to confirm the revised timeline and settle who owns alerts.' No trailing filler. Draft C closes on 'Reply with your availability and we'll get it scheduled' — action-oriented but lacks a specific deadline. Draft B ends with 'Can you share your availability for a short sync? Thanks' — the trailing 'Thanks' is sentiment after the CTA, and 'short sync' is vague with no deadline.

criteria_analysis.name: Lean Information Density
criteria_analysis.reasoning: Draft A is the leanest — every sentence earns its place, short paragraphs, no redundancy. Draft C is also tight but the 'I'd rather work this out together than hand down a decision' line, while human, doesn't sharpen clarity on the news or next step. Draft B has some filler ('I trust you both to sort out the best division of responsibility — I just want to make sure we're aligned before moving forward') that restates what's already implied.

criteria_analysis.name: Direct, Human Tone
criteria_analysis.reasoning: Draft A has situation-anchored language ('you're better placed to make that call than I am') and reads like someone actually talking. Draft C is also conversational but 'I'd rather work this out together than hand down a decision' edges toward performative humility. Draft B uses 'aligned,' 'sync,' and 'I trust you both' which lean corporate/boilerplate, and 'sort out the best division of responsibility' is padded.

criteria_analysis.name: Clear, Single-Owner Action Items
criteria_analysis.reasoning: Draft A names a concrete action ('meet Thursday by noon'), a deadline ('Thursday by noon'), and clear deliverables for that meeting ('confirm the revised timeline and settle who owns alerts'). The action lands on both recipients directly. Draft C asks them to reply with availability and come prepared — reasonable but no deadline and the ownership is diffuse ('can you each take a quick look'). Draft B is vague — 'share your availability for a short sync' has no deadline and no specificity on what needs to be decided.

per_criterion.reasoning: Draft A opens with a reader-oriented acknowledgment ('You're both deep into the current dashboard plan'), delivers the news, explains why it matters (affects sequenced work), then closes with one next step with a deadline. Draft C follows a similar arc but the acknowledgment is weaker ('Quick heads-up' is slightly sender-framed). Draft B buries the stakes — it jumps from news to asking for a meeting without clearly explaining why it matters to the readers' existing work before the ask, and the acknowledgment is a generic 'Quick heads-up' rather than reader-situation-oriented.

Draft A explicitly names the reader's disruption — 'This affects the work you've already sequenced — particularly anything that was scoped around the original feature set and timeline' — anchoring stakes in their world. Draft C mentions 'meaningful addition' and timeline shift but doesn't name specific reader-side consequences like disrupted sequencing. Draft B frames almost everything from a process/sender perspective ('I'd like us to connect,' 'I just want to make sure we're aligned') and never names what the readers will have to absorb or re-plan.

Draft A closes cleanly on action with a deadline: 'Let's meet Thursday by noon to confirm the revised timeline and settle who owns alerts.' No trailing filler. Draft C closes on 'Reply with your availability and we'll get it scheduled' — action-oriented but lacks a specific deadline. Draft B ends with 'Can you share your availability for a short sync? Thanks' — the trailing 'Thanks' is sentiment after the CTA, and 'short sync' is vague with no deadline.

Draft A is the leanest — every sentence earns its place, short paragraphs, no redundancy. Draft C is also tight but the 'I'd rather work this out together than hand down a decision' line, while human, doesn't sharpen clarity on the news or next step. Draft B has some filler ('I trust you both to sort out the best division of responsibility — I just want to make sure we're aligned before moving forward') that restates what's already implied.

Draft A has situation-anchored language ('you're better placed to make that call than I am') and reads like someone actually talking. Draft C is also conversational but 'I'd rather work this out together than hand down a decision' edges toward performative humility. Draft B uses 'aligned,' 'sync,' and 'I trust you both' which lean corporate/boilerplate, and 'sort out the best division of responsibility' is padded.

Draft A names a concrete action ('meet Thursday by noon'), a deadline ('Thursday by noon'), and clear deliverables for that meeting ('confirm the revised timeline and settle who owns alerts'). The action lands on both recipients directly. Draft C asks them to reply with availability and come prepared — reasonable but no deadline and the ownership is diffuse ('can you each take a quick look'). Draft B is vague — 'share your availability for a short sync' has no deadline and no specificity on what needs to be decided.

rubric_judge_result.overall_reasoning: Draft A nails every element of the user's non-negotiable sequence (acknowledge → news → why → ask), frames stakes from the readers' perspective with situation-specific language, and closes on a concrete action with a deadline and no trailing filler. Draft C is solid but lacks a specific deadline and its reader-framing is less sharp. Draft B scrambles the emotional arc, centers the sender's perspective, and closes with vague sentiment.

per_criterion.reasoning: Draft A is more specific and concrete — it names the CEO as the source, specifies the feature (real-time alerts), explicitly states the delivery date moves, and sets a clear deadline (Thursday by noon). Draft B conveys the same core information but is vaguer about the impact and less precise about next steps.

Draft A follows a tight logical progression: context → change → impact → delegation → action. Draft B flows well but the middle section slightly blurs the distinction between impact and proposed action.
Both drafts are grammatically clean with no errors or typos.

Draft A uses short, purposeful paragraphs that each serve a distinct function (context, change, impact, call to action). Draft B is well-organized but slightly less disciplined in separating its concerns.
Draft A's directness and confident tone make it more compelling to read; phrases like 'before it hits your schedules' create urgency. Draft B is pleasant but reads more generically.

Draft A strikes an effective balance between authority and respect — it's direct without being bossy, and the line about deferring the decision feels genuinely empowering. Draft B's tone is appropriate and collegial but slightly more formulaic.

generic_judge_result.overall_reasoning: Draft A is more precise, more direct, and more action-oriented, with a stronger voice and a clearer call to action (specific deadline). Draft B is solid professional writing but comparatively softer and less specific where specificity matters.
